entries.title: Simple Grilled Sardines
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Simple Grilled Sardines
entries.notes: Absolutely delicious. I added minced garlic and rosemary to the olive oil and let sit while grill got hot. Served over arugula. So good!

entries.title: Easy Sheet Pan Chicken
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Easy Sheet Pan Chicken
entries.notes: Easy and delicious. I used a 3-lb. package of local chicken drumsticks. Served with a salad and quinoa with corn and zucchini. Nice summer supper. I loved the mustard mix and then the panko crisp. I made plenty of the mustard-butter mix and put a little under the skin of the legs.

entries.title: Simple Lamb Curry With Carrot Raita
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Simple Lamb Curry With Carrot Raita
entries.notes: I too just made the carrot raita, which was delicious as a condiment for grilled lamb chops. When the weather is colder, I'll make the lamb curry.

entries.title: Corn Pudding With Roasted Garlic And Sage
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Corn Pudding With Roasted Garlic And Sage
entries.notes: A great dish when fresh corn is plentiful. I served a lot of food when we had company over and this was devoured. What I most liked about it was that I put it together hours ahead, and then stirred and poured it into the baking/serving dish and put it into the oven about 75 minutes before the entree was to be served, just as the recipe suggests. It was perfect. Corn this year is super sweet so even though I thought I had added a lot of pepper, to my taste it needed a lot more.

entries.title: Greek Stewed Green Beans And Yellow Squash With Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Greek Stewed Green Beans And Yellow Squash With Tomatoes
entries.notes: Wonderful with fresh vegetables! The vegetables were so sweet even with the lemon added. What I like most is that this dish can be made ahead of time and allowed to sit (it will just develop more) and then served at room temperature.

entries.title: Pan Seared Steak With Red Wine Sauce
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pan Seared Steak With Red Wine Sauce
entries.notes: I made the sauce to  accompany a grilled steak and it was excellent. I thinly sliced the shallots and added them to melted butter in a small saucepan. After browning I added a bit of cognac and did not fire it up. It evaporated on its own in an instant. The wine and chicken stock also almost completely evaporated. Kept warm until just before serving, then whisked in the butter and chives. Delicious.

entries.title: Baked Potato Style Red Beets
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baked Potato Style Red Beets
entries.notes: This dish was fun, pretty, and also delicious. I had a bunch of young muti-colored beets and cooked them as directed. Before serving, and still warm, I split them  and dressed them like a baked potato with butter, a dab of sour cream, and chives. They looked great and added some festivity to the meal. The skin peeled right off as we ate them. I think I would peel them next time prior to splitting.

entries.title: Corn Risotto
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Corn Risotto
entries.notes: Nice dish. I used chicken stock and, as many others reported, only needed about 3 1/2-4 cups of it. I used a shallot instead of the leek and added a diced green pepper with the corn. And I omitted the cream but I did use the chives. Mild in flavor, filling, and attractive.

entries.title: Roast Chicken With Maple Butter And Rosemary
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roast Chicken With Maple Butter And Rosemary
entries.notes: Five stars for appearance but only four for flavor. My husband discerned the sweetness (and disliked it) right off. I would make this chicken again but I would be very generous with black pepper in the glaze. I cooked it over broccoli, small potatoes, and large-diced sweet potatoes. I started the vegetables, dotted with butter,  in the oven for 15 minutes prior to putting a rack on top of the pan and placing the chicken on top of it. Veggies were great. Chicken was very juicy and took 45 minutes

entries.title: Chickpea Vegetable Soup With Parmesan Rosemary And Lemon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chickpea Vegetable Soup With Parmesan Rosemary And Lemon
entries.notes: Basically followed the recipe but used 4 cups chicken broth and 1 cup water. Very pretty in the soup bowl; the Parmesan-lemon sprinkle adds a depth of flavor; lovely dinner with a salad and a loaf of fresh bread. I removed the clove from the onion, the rosemary, rosemary sprig, the bay leaf, and the Parmesan rind before serving. I never add much salt to my food so I added it to taste at the end of cooking (both the low-sodium chicken broth and Parmesan have intrinsic salt and celery? does too).

entries.title: Shredded Red Cabbage And Carrot Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Shredded Red Cabbage And Carrot Salad
entries.notes: Attractive and refreshing. I didn't have the dill and while I'm sure it adds that zesty dilly flavor that only dill can impart, the salad was still excellent. And can be made ahead, always a plus!

entries.title: Roasted Cauliflower Gratin With Tomatoes And Goat Cheese
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Cauliflower Gratin With Tomatoes And Goat Cheese
entries.notes: Others at our Thanksgiving feast enjoyed this dish more than I did. I loved that I could make it a day in advance and that it travels well. But I didn't like the appearance of it on the plate and something about the taste--I think it was the cinnamon--didn't work well for me. I would make it again as I liked the roasted cauliflower and cheese idea (I used 4 oz of a soft goat cheese) but I'd go with a more herbal seasoning, maybe oregano.

entries.title: Italian Style Braised Rabbit With Rosemary And Mushrooms
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Italian Style Braised Rabbit With Rosemary And Mushrooms
entries.notes: Fantastic.  Beautiful, relatively easy to make, and can be made in advance. First time I haven't removed kidneys on a rabbit and we don't know what happened to them, i.e., if we ate them (and we must have) we were not aware. Anyway, I used 2 large leeks, 12 oz of fresh mushrooms, the mushroom water in lieu of the chicken broth, and 2-3 dozen cherry tomatoes instead of canned. Covered dish with parchment and then lid before braising. Served over pappardelle.

entries.title: Mustardy Braised Rabbit With Carrots
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Mustardy Braised Rabbit With Carrots
entries.notes: Delicious. I used a D'Artagnan rabbit. I needed to go out after I planned to put the rabbit in the oven so I turned the heat down to 300 (250 might have even been better)and covered the pan with parchment before putting on the lid (completely covered) and returned home 4 hours later to find the most delectable dish (what an aroma). I mixed the mustard with about another half cup of chicken stock to refresh and served over egg noodles.

entries.title: Chocolate Pavlova With Chocolate Mousse
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chocolate Pavlova With Chocolate Mousse
entries.notes:
This wonderful dessert is basically too sweet for me (and I decreased the sugar a bit). But I want to add something to the discussion anyways. I enjoyed the cake more the second day. The pieces, i.e., the pavlova, the mousse, the creme fraiche, and the strawberries, seemed more of one piece after a night in the refrigerator. And it cut better too.
I had some difficulty stirring the whipped cream into the chocolate in step 8. I did it but it took work. Anyone else have that problem?

entries.title: Roasted Butternut Squash And Red Onions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Butternut Squash And Red Onions
entries.notes: Delicious and very attractive. I used the wonderful Seed + Mill tahini and it's just a great product, much better than the supermarket tahini I've been using for years. The sauce drizzled on and looked so pretty along with pine nuts and parsley over the roasted butternut squash and red onions.

entries.title: Roasted Halibut And Potatoes With Rosemary
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Halibut And Potatoes With Rosemary
entries.notes: The halibut was very moist and the flavor clean and bright. Delicious preparation.

entries.title: Garlic Braised Short Ribs With Red Wine
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Garlic Braised Short Ribs With Red Wine
entries.notes: Delicious. For the two of us I used 1.3 lbs of bison short ribs (from the Readington River Buffalo Farm in Flemington NJ) and basically cut the rest of the recipe in half. Bison has virtually no fat so need need to separate the fat from the sauce. One trick I use when braising is covering the food with parchment paper, under the lid, prior to putting in the oven. It ensures the meat will not dry out at all.

entries.title: Italian Wedding Soup With Turkey Meatballs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Italian Wedding Soup With Turkey Meatballs
entries.notes: This is a perfectly good soup, not a great one, but certainly the best Italian wedding soup I've ever had (they are always salted beyond redemption in my experience). I made small meatballs--I think the pound of ground turkey made 24. The soup was fine the next day too topped with more parmesan and black pepper. It's a pleasant variation for chicken soup.

entries.title: Seared Scallop Pasta With Burst Tomatoes And Herbs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Seared Scallop Pasta With Burst Tomatoes And Herbs
entries.notes: Beautiful and delicious. For the two of us I cooked 12 oz of pasta and about 12 oz of scallops (7 beautiful scallops from Metropolitan Seafood in Lebanon NJ). I sauteed a small yellow onion instead of the shallots and my greens were a combo of parsley, chives, and spinach. With a green salad this was too much for two relatively big eaters. This dish would be an excellent first course pasta, 2 oz of pasta with a large scallop on top. It's a very rich dish and very delicious.

entries.title: Baby Bok Choy With Oyster Sauce
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baby Bok Choy With Oyster Sauce
entries.notes: Great and easy way to cook baby bok choy. Used it as a side to a beef stir fry. I omitted the sugar--used a little mirin instead. Next time I would cut the vegetable in half for smaller pieces. FYI: the supermarket brand of oyster sauce I used--Kame--does not list MSG as an ingredient.

entries.title: Stir Fried Beef And Sugar Snap Peas
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Stir Fried Beef And Sugar Snap Peas
entries.notes: Used this as a base for a no-recipe day because we had a good-sized piece of a rib-eye steak left over. I bought the sugar snap peas and and also bean sprouts but besides those 2 things I had everything in the pantry. I skipped step one. Made a sauce from mushroom soaking liquid (I added soaked dried mushrooms), mirin, tamari,  rice vinegar, and cornstarch. Stir fried pretty much as directed but also added water chestnuts and baby corn. Served over rice with baby bok choy side. No complaints.

entries.title: Salted Tahini Chocolate Chip Cookies
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Salted Tahini Chocolate Chip Cookies
entries.notes: Recipe made 25 good-sized cookies. I think I would make them even smaller next time. Used bittersweet chocolate chips and loved the flavors. Cookies are very soft when taken out of the oven (at 16-18 minutes) so don't try to take them off the cookie sheet until they have cooled a bit. I used an ice cream scoop to form the cookies and rounded scoops into little balls.

entries.title: Pressure Cooker Corned Beef And Cabbage
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pressure Cooker Corned Beef And Cabbage
entries.notes: Excellent recipe. I used Sauvignon blanc for the wine, added white turnips to the vegetables, and otherwise followed the instructions using a 2-lb low-salt corned beef. And I skipped the honey. I should have spread the mustard on the non-fatty surface but didn't think of that in time. We devoured this. The vegetables were perfect, the meat melted, and the dish, for the first time, wasn't too salty or sour. Just delicious.Perhaps this once-a-year dish should be repeated before next St. Pat's day!

entries.title: Farro And Arborio Risotto With Leeks Herbs And Lemon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Farro And Arborio Risotto With Leeks Herbs And Lemon
entries.notes: Wonderful dish. I halved it for a side dish for two and we devoured all of it. I used Carnaroli rice, farro, and parsley. I loved the appearance, the texture, and the taste. Would be wonderful with mushrooms!

entries.title: Fennel Apple Salad With Walnuts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Fennel Apple Salad With Walnuts
entries.notes: Refreshing, attractive salad. I made it ahead to see if the sliced Granny Smith apples would brown and they didn't. I sliced a knife and my slices were fine. I'm thinking of serving this for Passover along side the heavy entrees.

entries.title: Matzo Lasagna
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Matzo Lasagna
entries.notes: This is a wonderful addition to Passover cooking. "Matsagna" was new to me. Vegetarians will love it. And I love another main course alternative for the week. Anyway, I followed the recipe faithfully and it worked perfectly. I needed 1 1/2 pieces of matza per layer (matza generally comes in a 16-oz box). The recipe completely filled my standard 9x13 pan and din't run over although I was afraid it would. I assembled it a day ahead and baked it before serving. The pieces stayed together when cut.

entries.title: Citrusy Brisket With Spring Lettuces
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Citrusy Brisket With Spring Lettuces
entries.notes: Superb! First of all I had no trouble with the garlic burning. I minced it finely and when it came to searing had no problem. After the long cooking I refrigerated the brisket and the next day skimmed the fat off the top and sliced the meat thinly before reheating. I served the salad in a bowl alongside the meat and there were no leftovers.

entries.title: Braised Beef With Eggplant
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Braised Beef With Eggplant
entries.notes: I made this with a roast of venison. The flavor was wonderful. Made the day before serving, sliced the next day, and reheated in the oven. As there is next to no fat in venison you need to make sure the meat stays moist. Very nice.

entries.title: Marsala Marinated Chicken With Roasted Vegetables
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Marsala Marinated Chicken With Roasted Vegetables
entries.notes: Delicious. I love sheet-pan dinners! Chicken skin was crisp and meat was tender. Flavors wonderful. Vegetables wonderful. The marinade could have been scaled down a bit but you do want the chicken to be in enough liquid (I refrigerated about 5 hours). The salad was nice. I added quite a bit more mustard to the salad dressing. The chicken was finished about an hour before serving. I covered it with foil, added the vegetables when they were done, and kept warm until serving. Excellent.

entries.title: Pan Fried Halibut With Spiced Chickpea And Herb Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pan Fried Halibut With Spiced Chickpea And Herb Salad
entries.notes: This was delicious but I must agree that it is a bit too complicated for a quick dinner. Too many bowls. The finished product was fantastic, fine-restaurant appeal, and very flavorful. Next time I would make both the chick peas and salad ahead of time. My substitutions: sweet onion instead of spring onion; crushed red pepper instead of chiles; omission of lime or substitute; and labne instead of yogurt. 1/4 pound of halibut pp, which is so expensive these days, was enough. Excellent recipe.

entries.title: Bucatini With Fennel And Anchovies
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Bucatini With Fennel And Anchovies
entries.notes:
Superb. Tastes like Sicily and the sea. I used a short twisted pasta instead of bucatini, panko bread crumbs, and fennel fronds instead of tarragon. It needed little water at the end,l I think it was 2 shot glasses full. 
I really enjoy the recipes that use mostly pantry staples and this one is a true winner.

entries.title: Green Goddess Salmon With Potatoes And Snap Peas
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Green Goddess Salmon With Potatoes And Snap Peas
entries.notes: Delicious and somewhat unusual. The sauce will vary depending on the herbs you use. I added parsley, dill, cilantro, chives, and mint and a little leftover pesto to full-fat labne (Lebanese yogurt). It was too thick to blend so I added some olive oil. For vegetables, I blanched both snap peas and asparagus and served those with the roasted potatoes and cucumber. My salmon took a little longer to cook as it was a nice thick piece. This is a nice spring dish when these herbs are in the garden.

entries.title: Grilled Swordfish With Smoky Tomato Anchovy Salsa
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Grilled Swordfish With Smoky Tomato Anchovy Salsa
entries.notes: Fantastic. All I could want in a summer dinner. Delicious combination of flavors with very fresh swordfish (the quality of the fish makes all the difference). Cooked over a gas grill until done. Jalapeno instead of Fresno; fresh oregano instead of dried;a fresh salad instead of just arugula with a vinaigrette.

entries.title: Baked Orzo With Tomatoes Roasted Peppers And Zucchini
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baked Orzo With Tomatoes Roasted Peppers And Zucchini
entries.notes: This is a wonderful,attractive, easy-to-like dish. What's best is that it can be made ahead, reheated or not, and varied depending on what's available.

entries.title: Orecchiette With Corn Jalapeno Feta And Basil
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Orecchiette With Corn Jalapeno Feta And Basil
entries.notes: A nice summer dish when corn is in season. I reduced the amount of feta and jalapeno and added sun-dried tomatoes. This would be good as a side dish to a more savory main.

entries.title: Sheveed Polo Dill Rice
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sheveed Polo Dill Rice
entries.notes: Excellent. I soaked the rice for 10 minutes, then drained it, and put it in the pot with water and butter. I cooked it until the water was absorbed (about 10 minutes), stirred in the dill, covered the pot with a towel and then the lid, and then let it sit until dinner--inn this case the wonderful roasted dill salmon--about 1/2 hour with the stove turned off. I fluffed before serving and it was perfect. The rice was tasty and attractive.

entries.title: Roasted Dill Salmon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Dill Salmon
entries.notes: Superb! I pretty much made as directed and found the flavor of the salmon and sauce delicious. I cooked the salmon in a small glass dish lined with parchment paper after marinating at room temperature for 15 minutes. The salmon needed a few more minutes to cook through--maybe 15 minutes in all--and it was wonderful. I served it with the rice with dill as suggested and drizzled the rice with the extra sauce.

entries.title: Farro Risotto With Sweet Corn And Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Farro Risotto With Sweet Corn And Tomatoes
entries.notes: I really enjoy making and eating farro and this dish was an excellent way to use it. I think of farro as a winter grain but tonight I used it in mid-summer with a piece of grilled salmon. The flavors--the tomato and lemon--worked very nicely with the salmon. And the corn looked so pretty with it all (and using the cobs is always a plus when you have luscious New Jersey corn).

entries.title: Ratatouille
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Ratatouille
entries.notes: When I first made ratatouille I would cook my vegetables separately and then combine. For many years now I've been cooking them all together. This recipe takes me back to that original ratatouille. And it's so easy roasting the vegetables in the oven on separate racks. I agree with many of you about adding another tomato or two but all of the vegetables can be different sizes and it will still be fantastic when everything is fresh. You can garnish this ratatouille with different herbs and capers

entries.title: Marinated Venison Steaks
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Marinated Venison Steaks
entries.notes: I used this marinade recipe for venison chops, which were grilled after marinating about 6 hours. The result was excellent, tender and tasty.

entries.title: Grilled Fish Fillets Or Steaks
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Grilled Fish Fillets Or Steaks
entries.notes: Simple and perfect. Good halibut is just so good! Per another cook's suggestion I served it with a variation of the eggplant salad and it went so well/

entries.title: Spicy Shrimp With Blistered Cucumbers Corn And Tomato
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy Shrimp With Blistered Cucumbers Corn And Tomato
entries.notes: I halved this recipe for a side dish for two and it worked just fine, in fact, was delicious served with rice. I used more corn proportionately (it's in season and well, why not?), used a regular cucumber, and half a jalapeno pepper. I quite enjoyed the flavors.

entries.title: Roasted Zucchini With Garlicky Bread Crumbs And Mozzarella
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Zucchini With Garlicky Bread Crumbs And Mozzarella
entries.notes:
The flavors are excellent and the zucchini sticks are fun to eat. I had a large but tender zucchini and my pieces weren't even but it didn't matter. A little messy but that didn't matter. Instead of the olives on top, I spread a knifeful of a jarred tapenade on each one. 
I put foil down on my baking sheet so it would be easier to clean.

entries.title: Grilled Corn And Avocado Salad With Feta Dressing
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Grilled Corn And Avocado Salad With Feta Dressing
entries.notes: This wasn't the knockout I expected. I think it needed some color--like a few  halved cherry tomatoes. The grilled corn was delicious but somehow the parts didn't add up to a great salad. I halved the recipe and perhaps I needed more dressing. Also, a more flavorful green such as arugula would add interest and flavor.

entries.title: Cowboy Caviar
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Cowboy Caviar
entries.notes: This was totally new to me but I took a chance  on it when t was hosting a large gathering. It was an enormous success; they loved it and I had many requests for the recipe. My take: the flavors were delicious (fresh corn is in season) but it is messy to eat. I prefer a dip where you don't need a plate underneath for most times but if you're outside and messy doesn't matter, well, this is a true winner.

entries.title: Chickpea Salad With Fresh Herbs And Scallions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chickpea Salad With Fresh Herbs And Scallions
entries.notes: Easy and delicious. For a crowd I suggest making at least 1 1/2 times the recipe. I used a full-fat Bulgarian yogurt (because I had it) but don't see why a Greek 0% fat wouldn't work as well. The mustard makes the dressing.

entries.title: Baked Greek Shrimp With Tomatoes And Feta
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baked Greek Shrimp With Tomatoes And Feta
entries.notes: Yummy.  I served it atop a slab of a good bread so the sauce could just soak in. Needed a fork and knife. Excellent side dish particularly at the end of summer when the garden overflows with tomatoes.

entries.title: Spicy Corn And Shishito Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy Corn And Shishito Salad
entries.notes: Super dish when corn is just right (and abundant). Flavors and textures work well together. In response to someone who said preparing the shishitos was laborious, just line up a few across a cutting board and cut off the stems. Then cut crosswise. You do not have to seed them. It should only take a couple of minutes. Very nice side dish to a piece of grilled salmon.

entries.title: Tomato Risotto
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tomato Risotto
entries.notes: Absolutely delicious! For the two of us, I used a cup of arborio rice and everything else pretty much stayed the same. For step 3 I added one cup of hot water (to which I had added 1 tsp of Better than Bouillon--I don't think it made a difference however), and then with step 4 I added another cup of water. It turned out perfectly. The flavors were wonderful. My tomatoes were juicy and I did not bother to remove the skins. And it did cook in a remarkably short time with virtually no fuss.

entries.title: Prime Rib Hash
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Prime Rib Hash
entries.notes: What to do with leftover prime rib? Well, this makes for a wonderful supper. I sauteed the vegetables (step 2) in a cast-iron pan, moved them to a side, added the cooked potatoes to the other side of the pan. squished half of them, and  then formed a large "patty" with my hands right in that pan. That way it could go right into the oven after setting on the stove. Very tasty. I served it without the eggs.

entries.title: Olive Oil Braised Chickpeas And Broccoli Rabe
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Olive Oil Braised Chickpeas And Broccoli Rabe
entries.notes: This was good but not great. The aroma did fill up my kitchen and I liked that (some may not). I prefer sauteed broccoli rabe to this.

entries.title: Baked Apples
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baked Apples
entries.notes: Very nice. And, yes, comfort food. I used honeycrisps and somehow I wanted a little more flavor. I wish I could recreate my mother's baked apples--what apples would she have used?

entries.title: Farro Salad With Leeks Chickpeas And Currants
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Farro Salad With Leeks Chickpeas And Currants
entries.notes: Loved the complexity of textures and flavors of this dish. I didn't have leeks so I caramelized onions. I love a dish you can make ahead!

entries.title: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous
entries.notes:
Absolutely delicious with and without the yogurt. The flavor is spicy but not hot (I went easy on the cayenne). One medium eggplant was perfect. I served it with a roast duck and I imagine a roast chicken would also be wonderful with the dish.  As a side dish this could serve 8. Would be excellent for a buffet.
One note: it is very easy to burn the couscous when toasting it. I looked away for a couple of minutes and had to start all over again.

entries.title: Grilled Eggplant Peppers And Onions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Grilled Eggplant Peppers And Onions
entries.notes: Very nice as a condiment (salad) to grilled chicken. Use whatever herbs you have around.

entries.title: Salmon And Tomatoes In Foil
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Salmon And Tomatoes In Foil
entries.notes: This was excellent. I followed the recipe using the double-layered tin foil and roasting in the oven. So pretty. My only comment is that in the oven there is no way to know when the salmon "starts to sizzle."  I removed it after about 15 minutes. Interestingly, the package that "rested" the longest was the tastiest, as in a very short time the flavors just came together. Such a pretty presentation.

entries.title: Romaine And Radish Salad With Buttermilk Lemon Dressing
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Romaine And Radish Salad With Buttermilk Lemon Dressing
entries.notes: The dressing is a bit thin even with a full-fat buttermilk but very tasty. My salad was a CSA special: napa cabbage, two kinds of radishes, and hakurei turnips. The flavors went together beautifully.

entries.title: Fennel And Orange Scented Challah
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Fennel And Orange Scented Challah
entries.notes: First time using this recipe. I usually do the 5-fold no-knead challah. I cut this recipe in half and found it easy to make, and oh so dramatic to serve. I very much wish the the recipe was in grams as there are so many variables in measuring. My dough was actually dry with the 3 cups of flour and I had to add about 1/4 cup of water. The first rise was 1 1/2 hours. I pulled my strands tightly when forming the loaf and my bread was almost 6" high when done. Beautiful.

entries.title: Stuffed Squid Sicilian Style
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Stuffed Squid Sicilian Style
entries.notes: I loved the stuffing. If I were making this again I think I would serve a thick tomato sauce on the side or bake it in tomato sauce. My squid were large so I only needed one per person. Delicious.

entries.title: Apple Clafoutis
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Apple Clafoutis
entries.notes: Fantastic quick dessert. I used a 9" cast-iron pan. Turned out I only had about 1 1/2 lbs of Pink Lady apples and still the dessert filled the pan. I caramelized the apples, made the egg mixture in my Kitchenaid (using the whisk attachment but I don't think that should matter), poured it into the pan, and baked. I put it in a cooled oven few a minutes prior to serving. Delicious! Microwaved a piece for breakfast.

entries.title: Cucumber Raita
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Cucumber Raita
entries.notes: Excellent. I used a thick Lebanese yogurt and grated the cucumber. Held up very nicely for several hours before serving. Served with lamb.

entries.title: Skillet Chicken With White Beans And Caramelized Lemon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Skillet Chicken With White Beans And Caramelized Lemon
entries.notes: Lovely flavors. I read through the notes and sauteed the kale in the fat before adding the cannellini beans (drained and rinsed). This worked very well. I didn't eat the lemon rinds but they imparted a wonderful flavor to the completed dish (and they were attractive). All in all a very nice, relatively simple dinner.

entries.title: Tomato Sauce With Capers And Vinegar
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tomato Sauce With Capers And Vinegar
entries.notes: My last pound of summer tomatoes, grated (large holes) made a most delicious, and quick, tomato sauce. The texture was thick and lustrous. Served it with sauteed mushrooms. Can't wait until next summer!

entries.title: Four Spice Salmon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Four Spice Salmon
entries.notes: Delicious, attractive, and easy. I ground the spices and crusted the skinned fish as directed. I was very pleased with the results. I fried the fish in a combination of butter (not clarified) and avocado oil and served it with lemon wedges. Interesting combination of spices and the combination works.

entries.title: Sweet Spiced Mushroom And Apricot Pilaf
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sweet Spiced Mushroom And Apricot Pilaf
entries.notes:
Delicious dish but let me tell you know it changed overnight (and then reheated).  The flavor of the star anise took over! The texture of the rice was perfect--not too soft--and the flavors were good but intense. I had used broken star anise, not whole, and perhaps that is what accounted for the change as I couldn't remove it all. I'll make it again because I really liked all those mushrooms but I'll remove the anise. 
Also, to get the basmati rice to cook more evenly, soak it first.

entries.title: Pumpkin Bundt Cake With Maple Brown Butter Glaze
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pumpkin Bundt Cake With Maple Brown Butter Glaze
entries.notes: Delicious addition to the Thanksgiving table. I cut down on the amount of sugar (367 grams) and I think it was perfect. Not too sweet but sweet enough. The glaze is quite sweet. And the cake looked picture perfect. I did need to add a little water to the glaze to get it to drip over the sides (put wax paper underneath as you do that and then pull it out.  Also batter is spooned,  not poured, into the prepared pan. Wonderfully moist and beautiful cake.

entries.title: Pumpkin Chocolate Chip Cookies
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pumpkin Chocolate Chip Cookies
entries.notes: These were very good. I suggest putting the dough in the refrigerator for 5-10 minutes before forming the cookies. The batter will be much less sticky. And the Silpat cookie sheet liners work so well for a recipe like this. I got 36 good-sized cookies from this recipe and could easily have stretched it further.

entries.title: Quick Lamb Ragu
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Quick Lamb Ragu
entries.notes: Very nice supper with a salad and bread. I used about 1/2 pound of orecchiette with about 3/4s of the recipe and it was luscious.

entries.title: Osso Buco With Orange Herb Gremolata
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Osso Buco With Orange Herb Gremolata
entries.notes: Delicious winter dinner. I used beef shins from a local farm (I sure wish I understood all the cuts) and cooked them in the oven for a long time at a lower temperature. Served with polenta with mushrooms. I didn't have any olives and didn't miss them.

entries.title: Roasted Winter Vegetable Medley
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Winter Vegetable Medley
entries.notes: As a side for roast chicken, for two of us, I halved the recipe and cooked it all on one sheet pan, the butternut squash on one half of the pan. Delicious. Next time I'd add more onion and some garlic cloves. I turned the oven off after about 20-25 minutes and kept the vegetables warm while I finished preparing dinner. I love that this is what I call a minimum mess dish; just toss the foil and the sheet pan is clean.

entries.title: Pasta With Kale Pesto And Roasted Butternut Squash
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pasta With Kale Pesto And Roasted Butternut Squash
entries.notes: After my significant other argued with me about the definition of pesto (I had committed a grave sin by calling this kale pesto), we enjoyed this dish and I'd definitely make it again if I found myself with a supply of kale and butternut squash. It was tasty and very attractive. The flavors had an intensity that you don't get from basil and each stood out. Delicous.

entries.title: Swordfish Piccata
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Swordfish Piccata
entries.notes: Spectacularly good. I had my fish man skin a 3/4 pound of swordfish (that's the right amount in my house for 2--with sides) and cut it into 3/4 inch slabs. I used canola oil (my bottle of grapeseed oil smelled off so out it went) and basically followed the instructions. I deglazed with a good splash of white wine after adding the shallots and then added the capers, which I had soaked in the lemon juice. I swirled in about 2 TBSP of butter. Really delicious!!

entries.title: Vegetarian Skillet Chili
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Vegetarian Skillet Chili
entries.notes: So easy and so good. I used a can of cannellini beans and a can of fava beans, and served with cilantro, sour cream, and the pickled onions. What a wonderful lunch when I had unexpected company. And it looked so pretty.

entries.title: Old Fashioned Doughnut Bundt Cake
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Old Fashioned Doughnut Bundt Cake
entries.notes:
Delicious and easy. As for sticking, use the nonstick cooking spray. I had no problem at all: it slid right out of the pan. Nice texture, complex flavor, and not too sweet. A winner. I made it for the first night of Hanukkah.
This cake could serve a crowd and be served with strawberries and cream if desired.

entries.title: Spicy White Bean Stew With Broccoli Rabe
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy White Bean Stew With Broccoli Rabe
entries.notes:
Used as a side dish to pan-grilled salmon. Looked and tasted great. Used kale, 1 can of cannellini beans, omitted the parsley/cilantro and egg, topped with plenty of feta cheese.
 I usually ad-lib. It's the structure and method of a recipe that are important. The recipe is the inspiration and I go from there. That may take years of cooking for some but as they say, relax. If you're working with good ingredients, it will be good.

entries.title: Mushroom Bourguignon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Mushroom Bourguignon
entries.notes: Wonderful dish, filling, and quite beautiful over polenta. I used a mix of supermarket mushrooms and really any will do. The same goes for the wine. Ask into a wine store and ask for a wine you can use for cooking and drinking, i.e., relatively inexpensive. A salesperson will always be helpful.

entries.title: Crispy Parmesan Roast Chicken With Lemon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Crispy Parmesan Roast Chicken With Lemon
entries.notes: Delicious, flavorful roast chicken. I just used a little bit of salt for the overnight marinade and then brought the chicken back to room temperature before roasting. The pan juices were wonderful. Adding the Parmesan at about halfway was really interesting and worked beautifully. Delicious with mashed potatoes.

entries.title: Sheet Pan Harissa Salmon With Potatoes And Citrus
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sheet Pan Harissa Salmon With Potatoes And Citrus
entries.notes: Lovely and easy dinner. I used lemon instead of orange and added cut-up sweet potato and fennel to the vegetable mix (my sheet pan was filled, unlike the one in the photo). My salmon fillets were relatively thick so I actually roasted them skin side to the side. They were in the oven 12 minutes. I omitted the cilantro. What I most liked about this recipe was that I could do the vegetables ahead and then put the fillets in their place and roast later. The marinade (20 min) added great flavor.

entries.title: Sheet Pan Chicken With Sweet Potatoes And Peppers
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sheet Pan Chicken With Sweet Potatoes And Peppers
entries.notes: Exceptional. I had low expectations as my husband insists he doesn't like sweet potatoes. However, he was gifted a 3-pounder. For this recipe I used t about a pound of the sweet potato, doubled the onion and garlic, and pretty much followed the rest of the recipe. The cilantro was omitted as I didn't have any. The timing was perfect (have the chicken at room temperature) and do use bone-in, skin-on chicken (Bell & Evans is good). The onion condiment tasted great and was pretty.

entries.title: Farro Salad With Roasted Rutabaga Ricotta Salata And Hazelnuts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Farro Salad With Roasted Rutabaga Ricotta Salata And Hazelnuts
entries.notes: This was very nice. And I'm one who doesn't usually use rutabaga. Sweeter than most of my salads but very yummy. I used a combination of lettuces and napa cabbage for the greens.

entries.title: Pasta E Ceci Italian Pasta And Chickpea Stew
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pasta E Ceci Italian Pasta And Chickpea Stew
entries.notes: Excellent basic recipe especially good if you're having vegetarians over unexpectedly. I too added more water at the end and didn't drain the tomatoes. Very tasty and filling and attractive. With bread this can be a meal.

entries.title: Shrimp Scampi With Orzo
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Shrimp Scampi With Orzo
entries.notes: Easy, quick dinner. I found that the proportions and timing was spot on. I used a good fish stock (from Metropolitan Seafood in Lebanon NJ where I bought my excellent shrimp and they always label where it is from) and with a salad and a loaf of bread this was a great dinner (I'm still tasting the garlic, which to me is a good thing). For two people, I made the full amount of orzo with 12 oz of shrimp (unpeeled weight).

entries.title: Cabbage Potato And Leek Soup
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Cabbage Potato And Leek Soup
entries.notes: Very pleasing soup. Flavors were mellow. I used one large potato, low-salt chicken stock, and dried thyme. I had no problem getting the cabbage to caramelize on a gas stove in an All-clad Dutch oven with med-high heat. When I could see that the bottom of the pot was starting to turn brown I stirred. No residue on the pot and the cabbage and leek combo was excellent. The Parmesan cheese added another dimension. This was a delicious, comforting soup.

entries.title: Creamy White Beans With Herb Oil
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Creamy White Beans With Herb Oil
entries.notes: Quite nice as a side dish to roast chicken or salmon. I used pesto leftover from last summer to top it. Very tasty and easy.

entries.title: Wine Braised Chicken With Artichoke Hearts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Wine Braised Chicken With Artichoke Hearts
entries.notes: This was an excellent, easy week-night dinner but good enough for company. However, I think, that you should make two thighs per person and have some leftover rather than not have enough. For the two of us I had 1.5 pounds of chicken (4 thighs) with the two cans of artichokes plus a light soup, salad, and a side dish.

entries.title: Radicchio Salad With Crunchy Shiitake Mushrooms And Barley
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Radicchio Salad With Crunchy Shiitake Mushrooms And Barley
entries.notes: Delicious salad. I suggest using Bob's Red Mill hull-less barley; it takes a little longer to cook but it has a fuller flavor. I loved this salad although I had to use regular mushrooms instead of shiitake and I feel there was too much radicchio for the mix. The layering was a wonderful idea, the colors were vibrant, the dressing and herbs on top added so much flavor. I served it as a side to roast chicken but this could also be a lunch by itself.

entries.title: Vegetarian Chili With Winter Vegetables
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Vegetarian Chili With Winter Vegetables
entries.notes: Absolutely delicious dish. I pretty much followed the recipe, using 3 cans of rinsed pinto beans. I added a couple of cups of water during step 3, not wanting the mixture to stick to the bottom of my pot and then added some more water when I reheated it the next day. I found the melange of flavors perfect. It was a huge hit and serves a small crowd for lunch over rice.

entries.title: Skillet Chicken With Black Beans Rice And Chiles
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Skillet Chicken With Black Beans Rice And Chiles
entries.notes: Wonderful, flavorful dinner. I poured off some fat after browning the chicken and I soaked the Basmati rice for an hour before cooking. After assembling the dish and adding the chicken stock I brought the pan (a 10-inch Staub) to a boil before putting it in the oven. Forty minutes later it was all cooked perfectly. I don't usually use hot peppers but two seeded jalapenos were excellent in this dish. Next time I would omit the cinnamon. I loved that the various components could be prepared ahead.

entries.title: White Bean Piccata Pasta With Broccoli
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: White Bean Piccata Pasta With Broccoli
entries.notes: Very nice as a side dish. I used the full amount of ingredients for 1/2 pound of pasta and I still could have used more broccoli. Next time garlic and maybe Parmesan to finish with less lemon.

entries.title: Chickpea And Winter Vegetable Stew
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chickpea And Winter Vegetable Stew
entries.notes: Fabulous. I made it with most of the ingredients. Yes, check at 20 minutes. For two I used all the vegetables with a cup of couscous (and water, not vegetable stock) and will have enough for a side tomorrow.

entries.title: Slow Roasted Fish With Mustard And Dill
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Slow Roasted Fish With Mustard And Dill
entries.notes: Is it possible to give this recipe 10 stars? Absolutely delectable. The whole porgy weighed 1 1/2 pounds. I was quite skeptical about this method and kept it in the oven at 200 for 70 minutes until I got the courage up to remove the fish. The directions were perfect--the bones just lifted out. And the sauce was divine (I used yogurt). Porgies are cheap and local and underutilized. Wow, wow, wow!! Good enough for the fanciest of fish-eating company.

entries.title: Sauteed Baby Bok Choy
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sauteed Baby Bok Choy
entries.notes: I separated most of the bok choy leaves for cleaning. This also made it easier to cover with the oil. Served it with a whole fish. Very tasty.

entries.title: Crispy Potato Kugel
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Crispy Potato Kugel
entries.notes: Turned out perfectly. I grated the potatoes (2 lbs) and onion in my food processor, got as much water out as possible,added 3 eggs, heated a 7" skillet in the oven with the fat, removed the pan, put mixture in, and cooked for a few minutes on stove until sides browned. I then baked the kugel for about 20 minutes and removed from oven until about 1/2 hour before serving. Back in the oven until browned and heated through. Delicious. Turned out on a round platter, beautiful. Cut into wedges.

entries.title: Lemony Asparagus Salad With Shaved Cheese And Nuts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lemony Asparagus Salad With Shaved Cheese And Nuts
entries.notes: Delicious. Kind of a misnomer to call it a salad if you serve the asparagus warm. For herbs I used parsley, sage, chives, and a few sorrel leaves. For the cheese I used crumbled feta and I used pistachios roasted stove top in a skillet. I mixed all the herbs with the dressing. I loved the textures and flavors and thought it worked very well as a side dish to a veal chop. It might be a little too much to serve with salmon but maybe not. Certainly with any grilled meat it will be wonderful.

entries.title: Garlicky Buttered Carrots
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Garlicky Buttered Carrots
entries.notes: Delicious. I loved the hint of heat at the end. I cooked them a day ahead and reheated in the microwave. I used the chicken fat because I had it but another time would use butter.

entries.title: Lemony Shrimp And Bean Stew
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lemony Shrimp And Bean Stew
entries.notes: Loved this. For the two of us, I used 1.5 pound of peeled shrimp, the full 15 oz can of cannellini beans, and one leek. I used fish stock for the liquid. This was absolutely delicious served on pieces of a toasted baguette, which I smothered in the beans and leeks and put one shrimp in the middle. This would make a wonderful first course. I think the leeks were essential to this dish as they stay relatively intact and make the dish look and taste great.;

entries.title: Tangy Braised Short Ribs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tangy Braised Short Ribs
entries.notes: Fantastic recipe. I was lucky enough to have buffalo short ribs, which have much less fat and well, also taste delicious. I made them a day ahead, skimmed off what little fat there was, and reheated. I should have just warmed them at 325 and before serving or removed the ribs before reducing the liquid at 425. The sauce was wonderful. I recommend placing a round of parchment paper between the ribs and the pan lid before putting in the oven for the long braise.

entries.title: Spring Minestrone With Kale And Pasta
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spring Minestrone With Kale And Pasta
entries.notes: Very nice soup. Mine was so thick with vegetables it was almost a vegetable stew. I used asparagus, zucchini, peas, spinach, and whatever else was around. Excellent the second day too.

entries.title: Stuffed Peppers
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Stuffed Peppers
entries.notes: For the two of us, I used two medium-sized peppers, 1/2 pound ground lamb, about about 3/4 cup Rao's marinara instead of the can of tomatoes. Tasted great (topped with some grated pecorino locatelli). Served with a small portion of spaghetti lightly coated with more marinara and sauteed mushrooms. Peppers held together and were cooked.

entries.title: Pomegranate Glazed Lamb Meatballs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pomegranate Glazed Lamb Meatballs
entries.notes: I just made the lamb meatballs, not the glaze. Small balls, about 5-6 minutes about 6" from broiling unit (on high). They were delicious.

entries.title: Butter Braised Asparagus
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Butter Braised Asparagus
entries.notes:
What more can I say: my first bunch of asparagus of the season. Divine. Used parsley and chives because that's also what's in the garden now.

very nice. Used my first crop of asparagus. Cut them to fit the pan. Butter and herbs work beautifully. I will use ends for a soup.

entries.title: Lamb Necks Braised In Wine With Peppers
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lamb Necks Braised In Wine With Peppers
entries.notes:
I made this again but this time with a lamb neck roast. The neck had been boned and it was bound with string, like many other roasts. It was a bit over 2 pounds. I marinated it over night and cooked it for about 2 1/2 hours (with parchment paper under the lid of my Dutch oven to seal in the juices), cooled it, reduced the juices by a bit, sliced the roast (thickly), and added the slices back to the pot on the stove to reheat. It was utterly delicious and elegant on mashed potatoes and peppers.

OMG. If you can get a hold of some lamb necks, you must try this. I had 2 necks but they were not broken down so I was worried about cooking them whole. I marinated them basically as is in the recipe, cooking the onions and cooking down the marinade. I put the lamb in the pot and cooked for maybe 4 hours, turning now and then. I cooled the pot overnight and skimmed the fat before reheating with the peppers and also, mashed potatoes. Amazing. The meat just fell off the bone. And the flavors...

entries.title: Quick Tomato White Bean And Kale Soup
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Quick Tomato White Bean And Kale Soup
entries.notes: Very good. Another "kitchen sink" soup. And it is better the second day. I used a white turnip instead of the potato (a nice tiny crunch), one cup of leftover chicken broth (the rest water), didn't bother with the bouquet garni (just added herbs and removed bay leaf before serving). Used a 19 oz can of white kidney beans. Filling and tasty.

entries.title: Farro And Green Bean Salad With Walnuts And Dill
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Farro And Green Bean Salad With Walnuts And Dill
entries.notes: Delicious. I suggest toasting the walnuts in a cast iron pan prior to cutting them up. I substituted basil for the dill but other than that pretty much followed the recipe. I did cook the string beans in the frying pan as I prefer them softer. Flavors and textures were wonderful. I put the left over salad in the next night's green salad and it livened it up. Very nice.

entries.title: Greek Baked Fish With Tomatoes And Onions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Greek Baked Fish With Tomatoes And Onions
entries.notes: Absolutely delicious. I used a piece of grouper just under a pound for the two of us, a 14-oz can of diced tomatoes, lots of onion and garlic, omitted the cinnamon, and only a few sprigs of parsley. Baked for about 20 minutes and served it with a bulgur pilaf (with onions and garlic). Hardy but light at the same time. With a salad, a lovely spring meal.

entries.title: Sheet Pan Paprika Chicken With Tomatoes And Parmesan
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sheet Pan Paprika Chicken With Tomatoes And Parmesan
entries.notes: Delicious, easy, and pretty on a plate. I had two whole chicken legs, which I cut into drumstick and thigh. Put everything into a cast-iron frying pan and roasted. Served over couscous. If the chicken is at room temperature, it should be ready in 30 minutes but I always use a thermometer to check (it was ready). I shook the pan at about the halfway point. Basically, no clean-up.

entries.title: Striped Bass Or Mahi Mahi With Fennel Leeks And Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Striped Bass Or Mahi Mahi With Fennel Leeks And Tomatoes
entries.notes: This was wonderful, tasted like the Bahamas. I used about 3/4 lbs of mahi-mahi for the two of us, one fennel bulb, one leek, plenty of garlic, and the full 14-oz can of diced tomatoes. Served with roasted potatoes and a green salad. The fish was perfect, certainly not bland as it absorbed the flavors of the sauce.

entries.title: Broccoli And Scallions With Thai Style Vinaigrette
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Broccoli And Scallions With Thai Style Vinaigrette
entries.notes: Very nice. Roasting broccoli like this is just so easy and the result is very tasty. The sauce complemented it well.

entries.title: Crunchy Soft Shell Crabs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Crunchy Soft Shell Crabs
entries.notes: OMG. This was good. I too used some Old Bay seasoning but I don't think it mattered. We used canola oil. It does sputter so be careful. Too bad soft shells are so expensive (I paid $10 apiece and we ate 2 each). I served it with orzo with asparagus recipe from NYT and it also had a crunch and lemon. Yum

entries.title: Lemony Orzo With Asparagus And Garlic Bread Crumbs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lemony Orzo With Asparagus And Garlic Bread Crumbs
entries.notes: I liked the texture the bread crumbs gave to this dish, which is otherwise pretty bland. Next time, more garlic. Very nice accompaniment to fish.

entries.title: Roasted Cauliflower With Lemon Brown Butter
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Cauliflower With Lemon Brown Butter
entries.notes: Very nice way to cook cauliflower. I spread the florets onto a parchment-paper-covered sheet pan, poured the oil over them, and tossed. I think the water made a difference. Very tasty with the butter and sage. I cooked one good-sized cauliflower and used the few left-over florets in a salad the next night.

entries.title: Brussels Sprouts And Roasted Winter Squash Hash
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Brussels Sprouts And Roasted Winter Squash Hash
entries.notes: For 2 of us I cooked 1/2 cup of black rice. I roasted in 2 separate cast-iron skillets 1/2 pound each of cut-up butternut squash and sprouts cut in half, adding garlic cloves and salt and pepper to each pan. I cooked them at 375. The sprouts took about 15 minutes, the squash 10 minutes longer. I served all three components in a bowl with the rice in the middle. The dish looked great and the flavors worked well together. Served it as a side dish to grilled fish.

entries.title: Red Pepper Rice Bulgur Or Freekeh With Saffron And Chile
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Red Pepper Rice Bulgur Or Freekeh With Saffron And Chile
entries.notes: This was very good. I used freekah (my first time using it) and it does take longer to cook but I loved the texture (similar to bulgur). The saffron was a surprise and works nicely but I could see playing with flavors on this dish depending on what you're serving it with.

entries.title: Lemony Brussels Sprout Slaw
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lemony Brussels Sprout Slaw
entries.notes: This was a great side. I shredded the Brussels sprouts in the Cuisinart, didn't use the mint, but otherwise followed the recipe. I think it was a Gala apple; I shredded that too. Quick and easy. I served it with fried chicken. The next night I used it as slaw for tacos.

entries.title: Stir Fried Swiss Chard And Red Peppers
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Stir Fried Swiss Chard And Red Peppers
entries.notes: A nice, easy, and particularly attractive way to cook Swiss chard.  I didn't have any ginger so I added a spice mix that contained ginger at the end, and served it as a side dish to fish (rather than over a grain). I used a minced shallot instead of the scallions. As for the chard, the easiest way to cut it is to stack a few leaves together, roll them, and cut. For under1/2 pound of chard, I made about 2/3s of the soy sauce mix.

entries.title: One Pan Shrimp And Pearl Couscous With Harissa
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: One Pan Shrimp And Pearl Couscous With Harissa
entries.notes: A quick, easy, and very attractive dinner. Very tasty too. I don't like hot-hot and I was able to get just the right amount of spice to the dish. I used 2 medium-sized tomatoes instead of the cherry tomatoes. For the two of us, I used 12 oz of shrimp and 3/4 cup of couscous. With a salad this was a nice dinner. This dish would also work as a first course.

entries.title: Lemon Risotto With Summer Squash
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lemon Risotto With Summer Squash
entries.notes: Delicious. I used a Vialone Nano rice. For two of us, I used 3/4 cups of rice and went from there, using about three cups of a stock-water combo. I decided not to add the lemon at the end and threw in some earthy spices and it was wonderful. Served under a rack of lamb. Very pretty and easy.

entries.title: Baked Barley Risotto With Mushrooms And Carrots
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baked Barley Risotto With Mushrooms And Carrots
entries.notes: Very nice. I really enjoyed the earthiness of this dish. And very attractive. I used Bob's Red Mill "hill-less" barley. After 50 minutes in the oven there was still some water in the pan, which I spooned out, and the barley was quite chewy, which I quite liked. Flavors were great.

entries.title: Braised Chicken Thighs With Greens And Olives
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Braised Chicken Thighs With Greens And Olives
entries.notes: Nice, attractive, and tasty supper. I used whole chicken legs, which I separated into thighs and drumsticks, shallots, Swiss chard, red-pepper flakes, and omitted the raisins. The chicken drumsticks needed an additional 10 minutes to cook, which did not hurt the thighs as there was plenty of liquid. The flavors worked very well together. I served it over barley. Delicious.

entries.title: Greek Lemon Potatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Greek Lemon Potatoes
entries.notes: This worked perfectly for me and indeed rescued 1 1/2 pounds of "baby golds" that were on the soft side. After cutting them in half (not peeling) I cooked them in a Staub pan, used the olive oil, lemon, and chicken stock, and added some sumac, turned them as directed (added a touch more stock--I was so afraid they would stick as so many readers reported) and roasted them for about an hour. Delicious.

entries.title: Roasted Halibut With Lemons Olives And Rosemary
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Halibut With Lemons Olives And Rosemary
entries.notes: Well besides the fact that I completely forgot about the olives, this was an excellent dinner. For two of  us (with potatoes, asparagus, and a green salad as sides) I roasted 3/4 of a pound of halibut in one piece with the Aleppo pepper, lemon slices, and rosemary. Flavor was excellent. I did put my oven on broil after cooking for about 10 minutes for another 2-3 minutes.

entries.title: Sugar Snap Peas With Yogurt Feta And Dill
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sugar Snap Peas With Yogurt Feta And Dill
entries.notes: Absolutely wonderful. I used fresh, very young peas, radishes, and fennel fronds for the garnish. Looked gorgeous.

entries.title: Lentil And Orzo Stew With Roasted Eggplant
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lentil And Orzo Stew With Roasted Eggplant
entries.notes: I thought this was delicious and recommend serving it as a side dish with something full-flavored. We had it with a flank steak and it went very well (it would overpower fish.) I cooked it through step 4--the lentils--and as I was finishing preparing  dinner I added maybe a 1 1/2 cups water and the orzo. I quite loved the complexity of the flavors.

entries.title: Grilled Flank Steak With Worcestershire Butter
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Grilled Flank Steak With Worcestershire Butter
entries.notes: I made this with a bison flank steak and it was tender and tasty. I added some crushed juniper to the marinade and did not make the tomato salad, instead serving it with a lentil and orzo stew with eggplant. The Worcestershire butter was delicious. Lovely.

entries.title: Salad E Shirazi Persian Cucumber Tomato And Onion Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Salad E Shirazi Persian Cucumber Tomato And Onion Salad
entries.notes: Refreshing and attractive. I used an English cucumber and omitted the mint. Lovely summer salad.

entries.title: Roasted Salmon With Miso Rice And Ginger Scallion Vinaigrette
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Salmon With Miso Rice And Ginger Scallion Vinaigrette
entries.notes: This was a good, quick dinner. I liked it well enough but the presentation didn't wow me.

entries.title: Yellow Beet Salad With Mustard Seed Dressing
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Yellow Beet Salad With Mustard Seed Dressing
entries.notes: Delicious and gorgeous. I used brown mustard seeds and prepared horseradish. I will go out of my way this summer to get yellow beets to do this over and over.

entries.title: Grilled Porgies And Onions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Grilled Porgies And Onions
entries.notes: Great idea to char the onions (I did them on the stove because it was very hot today). Added fresh thyme and oregano (any fresh herbs would work). Fish cooked evenly on the grill. Served the fish whole on the table, not filleted with lemon on the side and the onions on top. Delicious!

entries.title: Simple Grilled Lamb Chops
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Simple Grilled Lamb Chops
entries.notes: Perfect. Local lamb cops schmeared with a garlic-rosemary-sage-salt and pepper rub; marinated for a couple of hours; cooked to perfection. Topped with charred onions. So simple, so good.

entries.title: Pearl Couscous With Shrimp And Clams
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pearl Couscous With Shrimp And Clams
entries.notes:
Gorgeous dinner. I had my fish store clean and devein the shrimp and it still took me 1 1/2 hours to put this dish together. Yes,worth it! I used the shrimp shells, no heads (not available). The flavors were complex and satisfying. There was nothing difficult about the recipe, just many discrete steps, but it is long for a weeknight but these days.... 
The comments prompted me to taste Pernod alongside Ouzo and Anisette. Anisette has an unctuous sweetness; Ouzo is harsh; Pernod is just right.

entries.title: Roast Chicken With Lemon And Zaatar
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roast Chicken With Lemon And Zaatar
entries.notes: Fantastic! I schmeared the chicken with yogurt in the morning, putting it in a zip-lock bag so I could caress it during the day; brought it to room temperature (about 1 1/2 hrs before going into the oven); made the za'atar mix (much more fragrant than store-bought); applied it; and put the chicken into the oven. Roasted 25 min. at 400, 30 min. at 350. Rested 10 min. Just delicious. Juicy, gorgeous, and so flavorful.

entries.title: Steamed Clams With Garlic Parsley Butter And Leeks
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Steamed Clams With Garlic Parsley Butter And Leeks
entries.notes: A wonderful summer dinner. I only used half of a stick of butter for 24 clams and the broth was rich and flavorful. Grilled slices of bread to mop up the juices.

entries.title: Shalom Japans Lox Bowl
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Shalom Japans Lox Bowl
entries.notes: This was fun, such a stretch for me, and so delicious. Used cornichons for the pickles. I had some problem with making the sweet kombu as I am not familiar with kombu and bought what the supermarket had: seasoned seaweed sheets. They disintegrated in the boiling water so instead I hydrated the dried seaweed flakes and that worked perfectly. What a treat. It's all about flavor and texture, not necessarily the "right" ingredients.

entries.title: Chicken Soup With Toasted Farro And Greens
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chicken Soup With Toasted Farro And Greens
entries.notes: Easy and versatile. I loved the crunch of the farro! I used 3 chicken thighs, skin intact, and cut them up for serving. I did not remove the bones. This gives it more flavor. I imagine that to reheat tomorrow the soup will need some water added. Very filling and delicious.

entries.title: Pan Roasted Corn And Tomato Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pan Roasted Corn And Tomato Salad
entries.notes: I sauteed the onion and corn in about 2 TBSP of butter and used red pepper flakes instead of the chilies. Wonderful salad. My corn (Hunterdon County in season) was sweet and this tasted like summer.  Delicious salad.

entries.title: Orecchiette With Swiss Chard Red Peppers And Goat Cheese
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Orecchiette With Swiss Chard Red Peppers And Goat Cheese
entries.notes: A great combination of flavors. I used small chunks of feta cheese and liked that the cheese stayed intact. Very pretty, easy, and delicious.

entries.title: Soft Shell Crabs With Spiced Cucumber Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Soft Shell Crabs With Spiced Cucumber Salad
entries.notes: I made the cucumber salad as a side to grilled soft shells. It  was yummy. Substituted red pepper flakes for the chili paste and used basil instead of mint. Very nice.

entries.title: Clarified Butter
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Clarified Butter
entries.notes: Perfect. I too used the solids to saute my vegetable side dish.

entries.title: Farro Salad With Corn And Crispy Chickpeas
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Farro Salad With Corn And Crispy Chickpeas
entries.notes:
Farro salads are just so good and this one is no exception. The crispy chickpeas are inspired (I had to hide them as someone in the house kept helping himself to them). Watch the corn--it goes to scorched rather quickly. And let the salad sit a bit before serving to let the flavors mellow. Tasty and attractive, not difficult although it has many steps, and the many textures make it a keeper.

Another wonderful farro salad, this one with varied textures and flavors. The crispy chickpeas are inspired. Watch the corn as it can burn quickly. And let this salad sit for a bit before you finish it with the chickpeas--it needs to mellow and be re-seasoned. A bunch of steps but not difficult and the payoff is very tasty.

entries.title: Spicy Cucumbers With Yogurt Lemon And Herbs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy Cucumbers With Yogurt Lemon And Herbs
entries.notes: I loved this with fresh, crisp cucumbers. Next time I'd cut them into 1-inch pieces. I used fresh oregano instead of the mint. Still good the next day at lunch.

entries.title: Miso Glazed Fish
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Miso Glazed Fish
entries.notes: Superb. Used a 12 oz salmon filet (skin on), marinated for 3 hours in about 3/4s of the glaze recipe and then grilled. Absolutely wonderful. Will try marinating even longer next time.

entries.title: Spicy Eggplant Salad With Sesame Oil
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy Eggplant Salad With Sesame Oil
entries.notes: Easy, can be made in advance, and will be good the next day. I steamed about 10 very small Japanese eggplants, omitted the ginger and cilantro. Added a jalapeno and basil. Delicious. Great on crackers.

entries.title: Provencal Fish Stew
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Provencal Fish Stew
entries.notes: A tasty and quick dinner. I upped the amount of shrimp and squid, used panko bread crumbs, and quickly pan-toasted the canned chick peas. Flavors were delicious and spinach almost disappears into the stew.

entries.title: Sweet Cherry Upside Down Cake
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sweet Cherry Upside Down Cake
entries.notes: Easy and delicious. Served with vanilla ice cream instead of the lemon whipped cream and a glass of Port. I pitted one pound of sweet cherries for this recipe and pretty much followed as written. I couldn't taste the black pepper but it must have cooled down the sweetness of the cherries. Check the cake at 20 minutes for this baked quickly. No problem inverting the cake from a cast-iron pan.

entries.title: Spiced Lamb Chops With Fennel And Cucumber
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spiced Lamb Chops With Fennel And Cucumber
entries.notes: Cooked the lamb chops on the grill. Salad flavors (fennel, cucumber (I used a regular cucumber) and lemon) with the chops are unexpected and quite nice. I basically used salad as a salsa.

entries.title: Summer Shrimp Scampi With Tomatoes And Corn
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Summer Shrimp Scampi With Tomatoes And Corn
entries.notes: Wonderful dinner. I served it over 1/2 pound thin spaghetti. When the corn and tomatoes are fresh, and you have access to good shrimp, nothing could be better. For 2, used 3/4 pound of shrimp and 3 large ears of corn. More than enough.

entries.title: Roasted Carrots
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Carrots
entries.notes: I basically made matchstick carrots and they were a wonderful side to a casual dinner. I didn't use any parsley. Attractive and the sweetness was nice with the skate I served as the main attraction.

entries.title: Roasted Corn And Edamame Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Corn And Edamame Salad
entries.notes: Delicious salad. Used basil instead of cilantro. Bought frozen edamame in pods and took a bit more time to assemble but worth the effort for the diverse textures and attractive presentation.

entries.title: Pasta With Caramelized Cabbage Anchovies And Bread Crumbs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pasta With Caramelized Cabbage Anchovies And Bread Crumbs
entries.notes: Wonderful. I made it as a side dish for 2. Having read through all the notes I decided to use 6 cups of cabbage and 6 oz of pasta. It was more than enough. I had no trouble cooking the  the cabbage down in 10-15 minutes (in a Staub cast-iron pan it's quick) but I had the time so just cooked it low and slow for an hour (covered to hold in the moisture). Flavors were excellent.

entries.title: Sheet Pan Chicken With Roasted Plums And Onions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sheet Pan Chicken With Roasted Plums And Onions
entries.notes: Delicious and easy. My very ripe plums disintegrated into a complex, balanced sauce. I served it over polenta flavored with pesto.

entries.title: Tsimmes Beef Carrot And Sweet Potato Stew
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tsimmes Beef Carrot And Sweet Potato Stew
entries.notes: Excellent. I served it over egg noodles. I browned the meat and then brought it to a boil with the water prior to putting in the oven. I used both sweet and regular potatoes and didn't find the dish sweet. I do suggest that the broth be reduced and thickened, by making a roux or adding Wondra flour.

entries.title: Miso Glazed Grilled Scallops
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Miso Glazed Grilled Scallops
entries.notes: Fantastic idea to glaze the scallops with miso and mirin. We grilled 10 scallops with this wonderful sauce. I forgot about the sesame seeds and parsley and served them over Ottolenghi's turmeric rice, to which I added fresh corn and omitted the parsley. What a dinner!

entries.title: Turmeric Rice With Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Turmeric Rice With Tomatoes
entries.notes: Delicious and so pretty. Put grilled scallops on top. Flavors are so nice. For 2 I used 1 cup of basmati rice and scaled the recipe down. Added (just because I had it) about 1/2 cup of corn kernels and they added to the texture and beauty of the dish.

entries.title: Escarole Soup With Rice
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Escarole Soup With Rice
entries.notes:
Easy and delicious. I omitted the cheese. I think adding tiny lamb meatballs to this soup and a touch of lemon might work very nicely.
When working with escarole, make sure to clean it thoroughly. I drop the chopped greens into a large bowl of cold water and then scoop the greens out, leaving all the grit behind.

entries.title: Roasted Salmon With Fennel And Lime
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Salmon With Fennel And Lime
entries.notes: A thick piece of salmon (about 14 oz for 2 of us) needed about 35 minutes to cook through and another 10 minutes wouldn't have hurt. Flavors were delicious and it was pretty. If I were doing this again I would probably precook the bottom layer of salmon before putting the salmon atop.

entries.title: Wheat Berry Tabbouleh
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Wheat Berry Tabbouleh
entries.notes: Bob's Red Mill soft white wheat berries give a great crunch. I soaked them  for a couple of hours before cooking. Not sure that made any difference but the final result was very nice.

entries.title: Linguine With Clams Roasted Tomatoes And Caramelized Garlic
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Linguine With Clams Roasted Tomatoes And Caramelized Garlic
entries.notes: Delicious. Left out mint. I suggest using a good-sized saute pan for step 3--cooking the garlic, wine, and then clams--and adding the pasta to it (no need to remove clams before adding pasta). I added a little of the pasta water to the pan too. Very pretty dish and very delicious.

entries.title: Pressure Cooker Osso Buco
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pressure Cooker Osso Buco
entries.notes: I used an old beef shin bone that I found in my freezer and really had no hopes of saving. It was spectacular cooked in my Cuisinart electric pressure cooker. The meat weighed about a pound (bone and all) but I followed the rest of the recipe. Enough for two with leftovers. The next time I see bone-in veal shanks I'm coming back for this recipe.

entries.title: Roasted Squash And Radicchio Salad With Buttermilk Dressing
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Squash And Radicchio Salad With Buttermilk Dressing
entries.notes: Wonderful salad although my husband didn't like that I used the roasted squash seeds in it. My first time using delicata squash and if you can find them (in the fall at farmers' markets) seems like a no-brainer. My salad was composed of radicchio, napa cabbage, and some random greens. Dressing was yummy.

entries.title: Miso Chicken
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Miso Chicken
entries.notes: Wowie zow. Absolutely marvelous and so easy. I will always have a package of chicken thighs in my freezer so I can make this dish whenever I need a quick dinner. Amazing! Starting from room temperature, bone-in, skin-on thighs took 30 minutes in the hot oven, time for me to make rice and saute some spinach. Amazing.

entries.title: Short Ribs In Broth With Squash And Shiitakes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Short Ribs In Broth With Squash And Shiitakes
entries.notes:
So good. Next time (and there sure should be a next time) I will put parchment under the lid of my Dutch oven  and put the pot  into the oven to cook at 325 for maybe 4 hours. Flavor was wonderful, just short ribs needed to be a bit more tender. I had a delicata squash but as they are not always available I imagine butternut would work as well.
Does anyone know how to make this in an electric pressure cooker?

entries.title: Braised Peppers And Onions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Braised Peppers And Onions
entries.notes: I made a batch to serve with steak. Added a large, sliced portobello mushroom to the mix. I enjoyed the cumin although I probably didn't use a full 2 tsp. Use any herb or spice depending on what you're serving this with, e.g., taggaron might be super with chicken.

entries.title: Pasta With Tomatoes And Beans
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pasta With Tomatoes And Beans
entries.notes: This was a great side dish. Very versatile and easy.

entries.title: Chicken Potpie With Cornbread Biscuits
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chicken Potpie With Cornbread Biscuits
entries.notes: Gorgeous. I had about 1/2 a fried chicken leftover so I took the meat off the bone (kept the crispy skin on) and progressed to #step 3. I used stone-ground white cornmeal. Biscuits turned out perfectly. Only thing I added to the filling was a 1/2 of frozen peas (for old-time sake). This made enough for dinner for two with enough left over for lunch. Really delicious and relatively easy. Would be dramatic in individual skillets with 2 biscuits apiece. Thanks for the gram measurements for biscuits

entries.title: Bay Scallops Provencal
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Bay Scallops Provencal
entries.notes: Beyond good. The cost of Nantucket bay scallops is astronomical but they were the best scallops I've ever had.  I bought 3/4s of a pound of these tiny gems and dusted them lightly with flour as directed, cut up about 2 dozen cherry tomatoes, instead of using plum tomatoes, and used plenty of fresh garlic. Made 1/2 pound of spaghetti, which I put into the pot after thickening the sauce, added about a cup of pasta water, and then topped with the cooked scallops. Gorgeous dinner for 2.

entries.title: Avocado Salad With Herbs And Capers
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Avocado Salad With Herbs And Capers
entries.notes: Lovely idea. I used what I had: first a layer of thinly sliced radicchio, followed by a layer of sweet onion, and then pea shoots. Topped with the sliced avocado. Dressing was red wine, garlic, and olive oil. Capers sprinkled over the salad. Very pretty and pleasant.

entries.title: Farro With Mushrooms
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Farro With Mushrooms
entries.notes: Wonderful. I used "perlato Toscana" farro, mixed dried mushrooms, and fresh shiitake and portobellos. This was delicious, with or without the cheese and still good rewarmed two days later. Very versatile, very earthy.

entries.title: Buttered Stuffing With Celery And Leeks
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Buttered Stuffing With Celery And Leeks
entries.notes: Excellent. I used cubed bread that my supermarket sells  for stuffing. This is a very standard stuffing that most everyone likes and sometimes the comfort-level is important. No reason not to put some of it in the turkey, in my opinion.

entries.title: Roasted Brussels Sprouts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Brussels Sprouts
entries.notes: I too cut the Brussels sprouts in half before tossing them with olive oil and salt and pepper. Put them face down on parchment on a baking sheet spaced apart;turned after 10 minutes; cooked another 10-12 minutes. They were wonderful.

entries.title: Gently Cooked Salmon With Mashed Potatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Gently Cooked Salmon With Mashed Potatoes
entries.notes:
Wonderful dish. Very attractive and flavorful. My piece of salmon was rather thick (for 2 of us  a 12 oz fillet) so I cooked it at 375 for about 15 minutes (and my husband likes salmon cooked through). Served over leftover mashed potatoes (from Thanksgiving), which I had refreshed with a couple of dabs of butter, The chive sauce was a winner and the plate looked like it came out of a professional kitchen.
Served a side of roasted Brussels sprouts.
Thank you all for your comments on temperature.

entries.title: Skillet Mustard Chicken With Spinach And Carrots
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Skillet Mustard Chicken With Spinach And Carrots
entries.notes: Quite perfect. For two of us (and it was more than enough) 4 small thighs and all of the rest of the ingredients as written (except for the dill) were used. Not only was this gorgeous but it was so tasty. I served it over leftover Thanksgiving stuffing because that's what I had but couscous, rice, even mashed potatoes would work.

entries.title: Pure Potato Latkes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pure Potato Latkes
entries.notes: I came to this recipe with an open mind. My husband makes the very best latkes and I was hoping to impress. The flavor was good; I liked that the prep is done in advance; however, without a binder (eggs) the "latkes" are just not going to stay together. Flipping them was an ordeal. While there is never anything bad about fried potatoes and the bits that fell off where sublime, I think we'll stick to our regular latke (blood-letting) routine.

entries.title: Maple Baked Salmon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Maple Baked Salmon
entries.notes: Delicious. Husband very skeptical; thought it would be too sweet. Not at all sweet. Complex. Only recommendation I would make is to use skinless salmon.
