The skin is not crispy and it's useless. I would oil the baking pan just a little before putting salmon in. For two, I used .8 pounds of salmon. Served with smashed potatoes and broccoli rabe. Oh, he likes well-done salmon, so I roasted it about 25 minutes. It was luscious.

entries.title: Butchers Steak With Leafy Greens Salsa Verde
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Butchers Steak With Leafy Greens Salsa Verde
entries.notes: Delicious. I had a bison hanger steak, about one pound. For the sauce I used one large leaf of Swiss chard (much of stem discarded), 4 scallions (2 in the sauce), and garlic. Cooked steak as directed and put it over the greens. Forgot about the lemon. Very attractive and delicious. Served with sauteed potatoes with onions and  yellow pepper.

entries.title: Crisp Gnocchi With Brussels Sprouts And Brown Butter
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Crisp Gnocchi With Brussels Sprouts And Brown Butter
entries.notes:
Delicious and easy and very attractive. Flavor was outstanding. I cooked the Brussels sprouts until I thought they were done. Even in a 12" pan they were scrunched together so it took a little longer. I was skeptical about cooking the gnocchi this way and my husband thought I had "burned" them but it worked perfectly.

Very attractive dish and very tasty. I don't know if I've ever used store-bought gnocchi before and I found them too salty for my taste. But everyone else loved it. I cooked the brussels sprouts over rather high heat and thought I'd burned them when I went to stir but the kind of burntness just added to the flavor.

entries.title: Soba Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Soba Salad
entries.notes: Rushed to make a side dish for dinner. Quickly boiled 3 oz of soba noodles, took them out of the pot into a colander with cold water running over it, boiled about 6 oz  of frozen  shelled edamame. Whisked in a salad bowl juice of 1/2 a lime, TBSP low-salt soy sauce, TBSP white miso, and 1/2 TBSP sesame oil (mistook it for mirin), and then about 4 oz baby spinach. Threw soba and edamame in bowl, added scallions. Delish.

entries.title: Strawberry Spoon Cake
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Strawberry Spoon Cake
entries.notes: So easy to throw together. (I used the Kitchen Aid.) Put it in the oven while you're having dinner and then serve warm with that dollop of ice cream. This is a perfect dessert for those who don't like overly sweet and/or elaborate desserts. Do use that toothpick.

entries.title: Creamy Mussel Stew With Peas Fennel And Lemon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Creamy Mussel Stew With Peas Fennel And Lemon
entries.notes: I didn't have the creme fraiche so I don't know how that impacted the final product but this was good but not great. The mussels were excellent but that's due to my fishmonger. The fennel just didn't add enough pizzazz.

entries.title: Baked And Sauteed Spaghetti Squash On A Bed Of Spinach
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baked And Sauteed Spaghetti Squash On A Bed Of Spinach
entries.notes: This is very good but a little strange visually: the squash sitting atop the spinach. I followed the recipe except for the walnuts because I felt their texture might distract. Roasting a whole spaghetti squash is easy. I doubled the bread crumbs as suggested and there still weren't enough to see them "color." They did add some body and the flavor was nice. Might be interesting to use flavored brad crumbs. Used as a side to a salmon fillet.

entries.title: Tahini Glazed Carrots
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tahini Glazed Carrots
entries.notes: So good I made this two nights in a row. As a side for two (for salmon/rack of lamb) about 3/4 lb. of rainbow carrots (from Wegman's). Didn't measure ingredients for glaze but more cayenne than I normally use for anything with the silan and both times it turned out wonderfully. Tahini  from Seed + Mill makes such a difference. First time using silan and was skeptical, given we don't like sweet, but this just worked. Try this with salmon or Arctic char.

entries.title: Arctic Char With Soba Noodles Pine Nuts And Lemon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Arctic Char With Soba Noodles Pine Nuts And Lemon
entries.notes: Lovely, light dinner. For 2, I used 3/4 pounds of Arctic char and 6 oz of noodles with all the sauce. To plate only used half the noodles and then we devoured the rest. Served with roasted rainbow carrots with a tahini glaze. Beautiful dinner. I used regular lemon and did not garnish with herbs. Really delicious.

entries.title: Roasted Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Tomatoes
entries.notes: I used these for the North African Meatball recipe. I sliced a dozen or so cherry tomatoes in half and tossed with olive oil, cumin, paprika, and salt as directed. Roasted for about 20 minutes until they were jammy. Delicious with the meatballs and attractive piled on top.

entries.title: North African Meatballs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: North African Meatballs
entries.notes: Fantastic and not onerous as so many of the comments led me to believe.The lamb meatballs were light, yet filling.  I left out the cinnamon (my husband doesn't like it) and the cilantro (I didn't have any) and used panko for the bread crumbs. Served over plain pearl couscous and adorned with jammy roasted cherry tomatoes. This would be a delicious appetizer for a dinner party (if those ever exist again). The aromas in my kitchen were so inviting.

entries.title: Dijon And Cognac Beef Stew
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Dijon And Cognac Beef Stew
entries.notes: I made this with bison. It was good, not great. I liked the mustard-flavored sauce but was annoyed that the carrots much longer to cook than noted in the recipe (luckily, I had read all the notes). Served over wide noodles the stew was a delicious dinner. I cooked one pound of meat with all the veggies and added more wine at the end.

entries.title: Roasted Brussels Sprouts With Honey And Miso
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Brussels Sprouts With Honey And Miso
entries.notes: Very nice. For about 12 oz of Brussels sprouts I used 1/2 TBSP red miso and 3/4  tsp of honey. Not too sweet in the least. Put the halved sprouts heads up on a 1/2 baking sheet covered with aluminum foil, roasted for about 15; turned the over and cooked maybe another 12 minutes. Nice flavor and texture.

entries.title: Earlonnes Chicken And Brown Rice
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Earlonnes Chicken And Brown Rice
entries.notes: Greatly enjoyed the story and the dinner I cooked. For the 2 of us, I used 2 whole chicken legs (Bell & Evans) and 3/4 cup of brown rice. I didn't want any leftover spice mix so this is what I did: 1/2 tsp of garlic and onion powders, coriander, and smoked paprika, and 1/8 tsp of cayenne. Only needed an hour in the oven plus final browning. Sour cream sauce delicious too.

entries.title: Eggplant Lamb And Yogurt Casserole
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Eggplant Lamb And Yogurt Casserole
entries.notes: This is delicious. Having read all the comments, I added 2 cups of stock instead of 3 and that was enough. Tasty and a nice change. I used 0 % fat Greek yogurt; I think full-fat or even 2% would have been better. And I didn't have a 10x13 dish so I used one a little larger. Next time the slightly smaller one. 9x13.

entries.title: Lemony White Bean Soup With Turkey And Greens
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lemony White Bean Soup With Turkey And Greens
entries.notes: Used spinach and 1 pound of ground turkey. Very nice and even better the next day.

entries.title: Crisp Braised Duck Legs With Aromatic Vegetables
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Crisp Braised Duck Legs With Aromatic Vegetables
entries.notes: Fantastic dinner. For the two of us I used 2 D'artagnan duck legs (dried and seasoned, overnight in the refrigerator), which do not have nearly as much fat as duck legs usually do. I seared them very well. I needed to add a little duck fat to the pan. Added two white turnips to the mix and cooked uncovered as directed. Did not take the meat off the bone. Pretended I was in a French bistro.

entries.title: Shrimp Creole
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Shrimp Creole
entries.notes: Excellent. For 2, I bought 3/4 lb large shrimp and served over white rice. More than enough for a wonderful dinner. Used a can of diced tomatoes and omitted the sugar. For the spice mix, as I only wanted to make only what I'd use here, I cut seasoning recipe by 1/3 but used all the vegetables and spices for the shrimp. The smoked paprika makes all the difference in the world and is reminiscent of New Orleans' flavors. Loved the topping also.

entries.title: Wine Braised Oxtail
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Wine Braised Oxtail
entries.notes: I used about 1 1/2 lbs of bison oxtail for this recipe. Basically cut the braising liquid in half and cooked in the oven 4-5 hours. Cooked and reheated the next day. Needed more liquid (threw in some wine and chicken broth), simmered for a while, and then served over medium-width egg noodles. Quite a delicious winter supper. The garnishes (gremolata and celery leaves) brighten the dish up.

entries.title: Meatloaf With Moroccan Spices
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Meatloaf With Moroccan Spices
entries.notes: Excellent. For 2 of us I used 1 lb of ground lamb and roughly 1/2 the spices  and herbs. Omitted mint. Baked in a 8" loaf pan a bit short of the 90 minutes (used a thermometer). I was able to turn it onto a serving platter and I quite loved it. The sauce was good but not necessary, as the meatloaf was very juicy. I would make it again. Served it with a roasted, spiced eggplant; recipe from La Boite site.

entries.title: Braised White Beans And Greens With Parmesan
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Braised White Beans And Greens With Parmesan
entries.notes: Very nice side dish. Tasty and filling. I used both the mozzarella and pecorino Romano and liked the flavor but found it a bit salty from the cheese. Can be made through step 3 and then briefly rewarmed and cheese added. Served with a juicy roast chicken.

entries.title: Skillet Chicken Thighs With Broccoli And Orzo
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Skillet Chicken Thighs With Broccoli And Orzo
entries.notes: Very nice, easy, one-dish dinner. I particularly liked the browned orzo. For 2 of us I used 4 small chicken thighs (maybe 1 1/2 pounds ) and 1 cup of orzo. Very tasty with one thigh leftover for lunch. I recommending not eating the lemon, so just push it to the side of the plate. It does add a nice flavor and is attractive.

entries.title: Seared Sea Scallops With Ginger Lime Butter
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Seared Sea Scallops With Ginger Lime Butter
entries.notes: I didn't expect to like the scallops-sweet potato combination but they went beautifully together. The bok choy was ok but string beans or another green would have worked just as well. Dish was gorgeous. My scallops were smaller so timing needed to be adjusted but they turned out perfectly. This is indeed an elegant supper. Oh, and I loved the ginger-lime butter. I grated the ginger on a microplane and melted it all in the microwave instead of the pan.

entries.title: Texas Chili
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Texas Chili
entries.notes: Delicious. I won't pretend that I know from chili but whatever I made was darn good. Served over rice. Used what I had: a pound of ground bison, a bottle of non-alcoholic beer, coffee made from Medaglio d'Oro powder, a TBSP or so of light brown sugar, half a jalapeno, a squirt of Sriracha (instead of the chili sauce) and all the other listed ingredients. Just enough heat but not too hot. Plenty left over for tomorrow. Tasty, filling, and I had everything in the house.

entries.title: Sheet Pan Cajun Salmon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sheet Pan Cajun Salmon
entries.notes: Wonderful. Thank you to all the commentators as I am salt sensitive and when I realized my Cajun seasoning contained salt I knew not to add any more salt to anything. This was a delicious, quick dinner. You do, however, need a sharp knife to make those slits in the salmon. For 2 of us, one .80 lb. salmon fillet plus 1 lb of small potatoes, and 1 lb of large asparagus, bottom half shaved.

entries.title: Lemony Chicken Feta Meatball Soup With Spinach
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lemony Chicken Feta Meatball Soup With Spinach
entries.notes: Quite nice. Flavors and textures all work. Used ground turkey, turkey stock (from Thanksgiving), and 5 oz of frozen spinach, which I microwaved prior to putting into soup. The lemon at the end tied it all together.

entries.title: Turkish Style Braised Eggplant
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Turkish Style Braised Eggplant
entries.notes: I loved this. Made as directed except that I omitted the cinnamon and used a good-sized handful of cherry tomatoes (didn't peel) for the tomato. The timing is off as many other readers noted. Eggplant sat in colander for 30 minutes but I hadn't allotted time for step 5, sitting uncovered at room temperature. Sat for maybe 10. Delicious. Loved it with yogurt. Served it as a side for swordfish.

entries.title: Salmon With Sesame And Herbs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Salmon With Sesame And Herbs
entries.notes: Delicious. Salmon was very light and flaky and the flavor was delicate but pronounced. Would be nice served over white rice.

entries.title: Pressure Cooker Kalbi Jjim
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pressure Cooker Kalbi Jjim
entries.notes: Traditional or not this was delicious. For 2, I used about 2 pounds of beef short ribs and about 3/4 of each of the other ingredients. Used white turnips instead of the daikon and a smaller amount of regular sugar. Cooked in the pressure cooker as directed in step 1 and then when cooled put meat and veggies in a bowl and refrigerated sauce and meat separately. Removed fat the next day and continued with the recipe. Served with rice and kimchi.  Meat was so tender and luscious.

entries.title: Whole Roasted Cauliflower With Pistachio Pesto
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Whole Roasted Cauliflower With Pistachio Pesto
entries.notes: Delicious. The pistachios lend a richness to the pesto that worked beautifully with the cauliflower and muted the cilantro (not a bad thing). I made this with salmon but next time I would use it as a side to something more formidable, I'm thinking lamb chops. And I'd make more of the pesto.

entries.title: White Wine Braised Rabbit With Mustard
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: White Wine Braised Rabbit With Mustard
entries.notes: OMG this was good. I used a 2.75 pound D'Artagnan rabbit that cost $30 and was worth every penny. I have local Pennsylvania Dutch rabbits available and they are much fattier and just not as tender. Before putting in the oven I covered the rabbit with parchment paper and then cooked for just over an hour, until the aroma just took over the kitchen. Served over pappardelle, highly recommended.

entries.title: Sheet Pan Scallion Chicken With Bok Choy
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sheet Pan Scallion Chicken With Bok Choy
entries.notes: Cooking chicken without the skin is a no no where I come from. So: for the 2 of us I bought 3 thighs (about 1 1/2 lbs) and browned them both sides  in a a little olive oil in a cast iron pan (Staub). I removed them, added the bok choy, which I mixed with the fat from browning the thighs, and then smothered the thighs with the mustard-miso combo before putting them atop the bok choy, put the pan in the oven, and 20 mins later 165 F. Served with rice mixed with mushrooms. Superb dinner.

entries.title: Roasted Asparagus With Crispy Leeks And Capers
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Asparagus With Crispy Leeks And Capers
entries.notes: Is six stars possible? Lined the sheet pan with parchment so no clean-up. Very thick asparagus, about 22 minutes (I could smell the asparagus-leek mixture); squeezed a lemon on them and served with sauce. As good as it gets.

entries.title: Tunisian Grilled Peppers And Tomatoes With Couscous
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tunisian Grilled Peppers And Tomatoes With Couscous
entries.notes: Great side dish. Ground caraway seeds in pestle and added a spice blend that had chile pepper in it, and garlic powder. Served warm as a side to a rib-eye steak (bison), forgot the olives, and was very pleased with the result. Just a touch of heat and that was perfect.

entries.title: Spring Pasta Bolognese With Lamb And Peas
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spring Pasta Bolognese With Lamb And Peas
entries.notes: Loved this. For the two of us I made the entire recipe (using light cream instead of heavy) and 8 oz of pasta. Served with a wilted spinach salad. Enough bolognese sauce for a second dinner. May add some red pepper flakes tonight. Very delicious dinner. Sauce coated the pasta (a thick Italian spaghetti) perfectly.

entries.title: White Beans With Radishes Miso And Greens
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: White Beans With Radishes Miso And Greens
entries.notes: I didn't have high expectations for this dish--I thought it would be mushy. But this was delicious and an excellent side for grilled salmon. I used a combo of arugula and baby spinach with two cans of cannellini beans. The two of us ate it all. I kept the heat on low when I wilted the greens and added a bit more water. Delicious and easy.

entries.title: Hanjan Chicken Wings
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Hanjan Chicken Wings
entries.notes: So easy. And delicious. I can see a crowd eating these non-stop. Anyway, I marinated 10 "Buffalo" chicken wings (I don't know exactly what that means but that's what the Bell & Evans package said). Used about half the amounts of marinade, subbed rice cooking wine for the sake, lots of garlic, and then grilled after marinating for maybe 35 minutes. Delicious and addictive,

entries.title: Pasta Primavera With Asparagus And Peas
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pasta Primavera With Asparagus And Peas
entries.notes: Very nice, anticipating spring, dish. Would be lovely as a small pasta course before an entree. I added about a cup of pasta water to the mix to lighten things up at the end, over a very low flame.

entries.title: Italian Red Wine Braised Duck With Olive Gremolata
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Italian Red Wine Braised Duck With Olive Gremolata
entries.notes: Quite delicious. D'Artagnan is now selling Rohan duck legs in packages of two, so perfect for us. Used about 2/3rds of recipe for the rest. Gremolata was excellent. As suggested I served lduck egs over buttered noodles to soak  up the wonderful sauce. I might have added some fresh peas or had a side of fennel. Use your judgment as to when legs are tender.

entries.title: Soy Butter Basted Scallops With Wilted Greens And Sesame
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Soy Butter Basted Scallops With Wilted Greens And Sesame
entries.notes: For the two of us, we had 8 beautiful dayboat scallops and 10 oz of spinach. Served over the rice (I added some sauteed mushrooms because I had them), this was a delicious and filling dinner.

entries.title: Farro With Blistered Tomatoes Pesto And Spinach
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Farro With Blistered Tomatoes Pesto And Spinach
entries.notes: This was good although not my favorite farro preparation. I felt that there was just too much going on in the dish. I omitted the cheese I don't know if that would have made a difference.

entries.title: Grilled Duck Breast With Miso Ginger And Orange
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Grilled Duck Breast With Miso Ginger And Orange
entries.notes: I turned this into a spring dinner for 2. One duck breast marinated over night. Salad: I added radishes, Vidalia onions, baby spinach, arugula, cabbage, whatever was around, to the mix. Cooked 3 oz of soba noodles. So noodles as base, salad (dressing was wonderful, added some olive oil and a bit more sesame oil to expand) and duck and mango on top. This would be a great summer dish around the pool. The duck looked burnt but was perfect, cooked over gas grill. More flames than usual but so good.

entries.title: Orecchiette With Cherry Tomatoes And Arugula
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Orecchiette With Cherry Tomatoes And Arugula
entries.notes: Visually beautiful. I used arugula and ramps (the bulb with the garlic and the leaves with the arugula) and topped with fresh basil and shaved Parmesan. This was a stunning side dish to a grilled veal chop. Delicious.

entries.title: Garlic Chicken With Guasacaca Sauce
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Garlic Chicken With Guasacaca Sauce
entries.notes: Fantastic recipe. For the two of us, I used four Bell & Evans chicken thighs (one left over for tomorrow's lunch), about 1/2 pound fresh carrots, 1/2 lb small potatoes, and about 1/4 lb asparagus (added about 10 minutes before rest of dish was done). All roasted on aluminum foil on sheet pan (no clean-up). I used 2 small avocados and half of a seeded jalapeno (can't handle hot) for the sauce. Finished plate with a few cherry tomatoes. Wonderful dinner!!

entries.title: Black Rice And Red Lentil Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Black Rice And Red Lentil Salad
entries.notes: I liked all the components of this salad and all of the textures  but didn't feel it added up to anything I wanted to eat again. Perhaps if I had dressed it an hour or so before serving and let it evolve. I used Venere black rice, which I leaned is a variant of the Chinese forbidden rice.

entries.title: Sesame Snap Pea Chicken Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sesame Snap Pea Chicken Salad
entries.notes: I made this with left-overs from a whole grilled chicken. I used about 3 oz of shredded chicken, 3 oz of  sugar snap peas (freshly picked) and maybe 2/3s of the rest of the ingredients plus a bit more sesame oil just before serving. I had it for 2 small lunches. Even better the second day.
